title: File Transfer Protocol
description: Protocol for transferring files between client and server over a network.
fullExplanation: FTP uses two separate TCP connections: a control connection (port 21) for commands and a data connection (port 20 or ephemeral) for file transfers. This dual-channel design causes problems with firewalls and NAT. FTP has two modes: Active (server connects back to client on port 20 — blocked by most firewalls) and Passive (client connects to server on a random high port — firewall-friendly). FTP sends credentials in plaintext — use SFTP or FTPS instead.

modes.name: Active Mode
modes.description: Client sends PORT command with its IP:port. Server connects back to client on port 20.
modes.problem: Client firewall blocks incoming connection from server. Rarely works through NAT.

modes.name: Passive Mode (PASV)
modes.description: Client sends PASV command. Server opens random high port and tells client to connect to it.
modes.benefit: Client initiates both connections — works through NAT and firewalls.

secureAlternatives.name: SFTP
secureAlternatives.description: SSH File Transfer Protocol — encrypted, single connection, most recommended

secureAlternatives.name: FTPS
secureAlternatives.description: FTP over TLS — adds TLS encryption to FTP (implicit or explicit)

secureAlternatives.name: SCP
secureAlternatives.description: Secure Copy Protocol — simple file copy over SSH

secureAlternatives.name: HTTPS
secureAlternatives.description: File download/upload via web (REST APIs, presigned URLs)

keyFacts: FTP sends username and password in plaintext — never use on untrusted networks
Two connections: control (port 21) + data (port 20 or random)
Passive mode is firewall-friendly — client initiates both connections
Anonymous FTP: login with username 'anonymous', any email as password
SFTP (port 22) is completely different from FTP — it's SSH-based
FTPS adds TLS to FTP — two variants: explicit (STARTTLS on 21) and implicit (port 990)
FTP is still widely used in legacy systems and web hosting

versions.feature: SSH File Transfer Protocol (not related to FTP)
